title: Sausage sandwich
subtitle: Grilled pork sausages in soft white bread with ketchup or brown sauce
excerpt: The sausage sandwich is one of the best uses of a pork sausage. Grilled until the skin is tight and slightly caramelised, tucked into white bread with a slick of butter and a generous squeeze of ketchup or brown sauce.
sourceNotes: Standard British breakfast and lunch food. The sausage sandwich is found in every greasy spoon cafe, motorway service station, and home kitchen. Cooked sausages in bread have been a British working-class staple since at least the early 20th century.

content.text: A sausage sandwich is best made with good pork sausages cooked slowly in a dry pan until the skins go tight and the colour deepens. White bread, butter, and the condiment of your choice are all that stand between you and a very satisfying meal. Ketchup is the usual choice; brown sauce is the alternative.
Place the sausages in a cold frying pan and set over a medium-low heat. Cook for 15 to 20 minutes, turning every few minutes, until evenly browned on all sides and cooked through. Do not prick them.
Butter the bread generously. Place three sausages on two slices, add your condiment of choice, and top with the remaining bread. Press lightly and eat immediately.

items.symptom: The sausages split and lose their juices
items.cause: Cooked at too high a heat, or pricked before cooking.
items.fix: Start in a cold pan over a low to medium heat. Never prick sausages unless the packet specifically says to.

items.symptom: The sausages are cooked on the outside but raw in the middle
items.cause: Cooked too quickly at too high a heat.
items.fix: Keep the heat at medium-low and give them time. 15 to 20 minutes is correct for a standard pork sausage.

items.cause: Sausages were too hot when placed on the bread, or too much sauce was added.
items.fix: Let the sausages rest for a minute off the heat before building the sandwich.

content.text: The sausage sandwich is British breakfast food at its most direct. It exists in every greasy spoon cafe along every high street, at every motorway service station, and in millions of home kitchens on Saturday mornings. The question of ketchup versus brown sauce is one the British treat with more seriousness than it strictly deserves, but the answer depends entirely on personal loyalty.
